aliases: Gyudon with Egg and Miso Soup

english: Gyudon with Egg and Miso Soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef bowl / gyudon entry; the combo format (with egg and miso soup) is not individually coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef dish — mixed rice-based beef dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated gyudon combo code. A0CKP (beef dish, mixed) is the closest defensible anchor for a simmered beef-over-rice preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for gyudon or Japanese beef bowl dishes as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 set meal (beef bowl, half-boiled egg, miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard gyudon combo set meal weighing approximately 550 g, comprising a beef rice bowl with half-boiled egg and a choice of miso soup or a beverage.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, all grades, cooked, broiled; White Rice, cooked; Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled; Miso soup, prepared; composite estimation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for gyudon combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked beef, cooked Japanese-style short-grain rice, hard/soft-boiled egg, and miso soup. Values are plausible for the described preparation. Confidence is medium. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several micronutrients are set to -1 where USDA composite data for this specific dish combination is not directly available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; White rice, boiled (Japanese short-grain) as analogous GI anchor
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for gyudon combo as a composite dish. GI of 72 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled Japanese short-grain white rice (GI ~72, high category), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The beef, egg, and miso soup components contribute negligible carbohydrate and are expected to moderately attenuate the overall GI; however, the rice dominates the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (72 × 89.98 g) / 100 ≈ 64.8. Clinicians should use this full-serving GL for dietary counselling.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100 g from soy sauce, miso paste, and dashi; estimated potassium below 200 mg/100 g provides insufficient offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and low-sodium miso, limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake, and pair the meal with a potassium-rich side such as edamame or spinach to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI Japanese short-grain rice (GI ~72) contributes 16.4 g carbohydrate/100 g with only 0.7 g dietary fibre; mirin and sugar in the beef sauce add to glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or substitute with a lower-GI grain, ask for reduced mirin and sugar in the beef sauce, and retain the beef and egg components as protein sources that help attenuate postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.18 g/100 g and cholesterol 36.36 mg/100 g from beef and egg; trans fat 0.09 g/100 g present from beef; sodium 327 mg/100 g elevates blood pressure risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to limit saturated fat and cholesterol intake, request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium, and consider substituting thinly sliced beef with a leaner protein such as chicken breast or tofu to reduce saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (beef) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways; insufficient anti-inflammatory counterbalance from dish components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu, retain ginger and miso components for their anti-inflammatory and fermented-food benefits, and increase omega-3 intake through a side of oily fish or flaxseed to counterbalance the omega-6 load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gyudon Combo is a Japanese beef bowl set meal served in Singapore, consisting of thinly sliced beef simmered in a sweet and savoury shoyu (soy sauce)-based sauce with mirin, sugar, dashi, and onion, served over steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, accompanied by a half-boiled egg and a choice of miso soup or a drink. The beef is tender and umami-rich from the soy-mirin braising liquid; the rice is soft and slightly sticky; the half-boiled egg adds creaminess and additional protein. Miso soup is prepared with dashi stock and miso paste, providing a warm, fermented, savoury accompaniment. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 136 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 5.1 g fat (2.2 g saturated), 16.4 g carbohydrate, 2.7 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 327 mg sodium. The high sodium content from soy sauce, miso, and dashi makes this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension. The high-GI Japanese short-grain rice combined with mirin and sugar in the sauce creates a significant glycaemic load, making portion control critical for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The saturated fat and cholesterol from beef and egg are moderate concerns for cardiovascular health. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy, egg, wheat, or fish allergies. It is a popular fast-casual Japanese comfort food widely available in Singapore food courts and Japanese restaurant chains.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from an ai_generated input record with confidence 0.7, cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component entries for cooked beef, Japanese short-grain rice, soft-boiled egg, and miso soup. No single approved-source entry for gyudon combo as a composite dish exists. Values are plausible but estimated. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled Japanese short-grain white rice.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, potassium, and extended micronutrient data for this specific gyudon combo composite dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100 g is retained from the input record; naturally occurring trans fat (conjugated linoleic acid) is present in ruminant beef at low levels, consistent with this value. All core macros (protein_g 6.36, fat_g 5.09, carbohydrate_g 16.36) are non-zero and consistent with a beef-rice-egg composite dish; no zero-macro justification is required. Glycemic load per serving (full 550 g portion) is approximately 64.8 GL units. Clinicians should use the full-serving GL of ~64.8 for dietary counselling of the complete combo meal. Local-language names added for Chinese (simplified: 牛肉饭套餐, traditional: 牛肉飯套餐), Malay (Set Mangkuk Daging Lembu Jepun), Tamil (ஜப்பானிய மாட்டிறைச்சி கிண்ண தொகுப்பு), and Hindi (जापानी बीफ बाउल कॉम्बो) to satisfy G6 multilingual naming compliance for this dish served in Singapore. These names represent the closest natural-language equivalents for a Japanese beef bowl combo set meal in each required language.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1-2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving to reflect full 550g serving.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory local-language names for Chinese simplified (牛肉饭套餐), Chinese traditional (牛肉飯套餐), Malay (Set Mangkuk Daging Lembu Jepun), Tamil (ஜப்பானிய மாட்டிறைச்சி கிண்ண தொகுப்பு), and Hindi (जापानी बीफ बाउल कॉम्बो) to local_names to satisfy G6 hard gate for a dish served in Singapore. Added all five new local-language strings to flat aliases array and to aliases_by_language under their respective language keys to satisfy Q8 and Q1.
